one_line_takeaway: ST-WebAgentBench shows that web agents which look competent by completion rate (24.3%) are far less trustworthy once policy compliance is scored (CuP 15.0%), with failures concentrated in user-consent and strict-execution and worsening sharply as policy load grows.
key_finding: Raw completion badly overstates enterprise readiness. Across the three agents mean Completion Rate is 24.3% but mean CuP falls to 15.0%, a ~38% relative drop -- i.e. roughly 38% of completed tasks violate at least one policy, so only ~62% of nominal completions are actually policy-compliant. Per agent: AWM has the highest partial-completion (PCR 46.9%) but only 20% CuP with 37 consent breaches; WorkArena-Legacy 26% CR / 15% CuP; WebVoyager worst at 12.8% CR / 10.3% CuP. The strict all-pass@3 (success AND compliance every run) collapses to 5.0% (AWM), 3.0% (WorkArena), 2.0% (WebVoyager), showing run-to-run brittleness. User-consent and strict-execution dominate the risk distribution (~70% of violations concentrate there). Ablations: full-set CR-vs-CuP correlation is a weak 0.48; removing strict-execution (+0.15) or consent (+0.13) raises it most, confirming these two dimensions carry the greatest weight. Scalability: while raw CR is nearly flat across policy-load bins, CuP decays sharply from 18.2% (one active policy) to 7.1% (five or more), with a strong negative correlation (Spearman rho = -0.71, p<0.001), implying real enterprise settings with dozens of concurrent policies will be far worse.
core_contribution: ST-WebAgentBench is a benchmark and evaluation framework (built on WebArena via BrowserGym) that measures whether autonomous web agents complete tasks SAFELY and in a policy-compliant way, not merely whether they finish. Its own novel artifacts are: (1) 375 policy-enriched tasks paired with 3,057 concrete policy instances across three enterprise apps (GitLab, ShoppingAdmin, SuiteCRM); (2) six orthogonal safety-and-trustworthiness (ST) dimensions -- User Consent, Boundary & Scope, Strict Execution, Hierarchy Adherence, Robustness & Security, Error Handling -- derived from an incident-review seed of 10 dimensions plus interviews with 10 enterprise stakeholders (the six explain 95% of cited incident causes); (3) an org>user>task policy hierarchy formalized as an action-set restriction; (4) new policy-aware metrics, chiefly Completion-under-Policy (CuP) which credits a task only if it is fully completed AND incurs zero policy violations, plus pCuP (partial) and per-dimension Risk Ratio; and (5) reusable YAML/JSON policy templates and evaluation functions (e.g. is_ask_the_user, is_input_hallucination, is_sensitive_data) plus a Modality Challenge that disentangles vision-only vs DOM-only perception. Note: prior controllers such as SteP, Agent-E, and GuardAgent are cited Related Work, NOT this paper's contribution -- the paper's novelty is an application-agnostic EVALUATION layer that scores every policy violation, whereas those are agent/controller systems.
threat_model: The benchmark targets safety and trustworthiness failures of LLM-driven web agents operating in enterprise workflows, rather than a single adversary. In-scope harms include: irreversible/unintended actions (e.g. deleting the wrong record), fabricated/hallucinated data (e.g. inventing an email or bank account), acting without required user consent, boundary/scope violations (accessing unauthorized areas like financial reports), leakage or misuse of sensitive data (e.g. a user's personal phone number), and violation of the organizational policy hierarchy. The Robustness & Security dimension specifically models an adversary embedding hidden prompt-injection/jailbreak instructions inside page fields (e.g. a malicious 'Job Title' value); the paper says the attacker model and trust boundaries for this dimension are formalized in Appendix G.1, and it cites Kumar et al. (2024) that refusal-trained LLMs can be jailbroken once embedded as browser controllers. The paper is explicit that robustness checks cover prompt-injection only, not the full adversarial landscape.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: only THREE open agents were evaluated, all sharing a single gpt-4o backbone; no proprietary computer-use stacks (Claude, Gemini 2.5, Kimi) because they lack stable BrowserGym integration, so this is not an exhaustive leaderboard. The 375 tasks span only three applications and English-language interactions, covering a limited slice of real workflows (early-stage domain coverage). Robustness checks focus on prompt-injection rather than the full adversarial landscape. The six ST dimensions encode one specific priority set under a single org>user>task hierarchy. pass@k was used due to substantial frontier-LLM API costs. Additional reviewer-visible caveats: benchmark class imbalance (GitLab dominates with 197 of 375 tasks; ShoppingAdmin has only 8), user-consent enforcement relies on fuzzy string matching against a template (potential false pass/fail), and the consent mechanism auto-approves rather than testing real human-in-the-loop dynamics. The paper frames itself as a foundation, not a deployment gatekeeper.

datasets_used: ST-WebAgentBench (this paper's own suite): 375 tasks, 3,057 policy instances, six ST dimensions
GitLab (from WebArena AMI): 197 tasks, avg 7.8 policies/task
ShoppingAdmin (from WebArena AMI): 8 tasks, avg 8.1 policies/task
SuiteCRM (added by authors, run locally in Docker): General 30 tasks + Easy/Medium/Hard tiers of 20 each + 80 Modality Challenge tasks (40 Vision-Advantage, 40 DOM-Advantage)
quantitative_results: Mean Completion Rate across 3 agents: 24.3%; mean CuP: 15.0% (~38% relative drop; ~62% of completions policy-compliant)
AWM: CR 33.8%, CuP 20%, PCR 46.9%, 37 consent breaches, all-pass@3 5.0%
Scalability: CuP 18.2% (1 active policy) -> 7.1% (>=5 policies); Spearman rho = -0.71, p<0.001; raw CR nearly flat (rho = -0.14)
Per-task risk ratio grows ~linearly with enforced templates (slope 0.11 +/- 0.02)
Ablation: full-set CR-vs-CuP Pearson rho = 0.48; delta rho when omitting a dimension: strict +0.15, consent +0.13, security +0.09, hierarchy +0.07, error +0.03, boundary +0.02
~70% of all violations concentrated in user-consent and strict-execution dimensions
Six ST dimensions explain 95% of cited enterprise incident causes
Total policy instances by dimension: Boundary 1,120; Strict Exec 959; Security & Robustness 386; User Consent 322; Hierarchy 152; Error Handling 118
baselines_compared: AgentWorkflowMemory (AWM) -- reported 35.5% success on WebArena leaderboard; gpt-4o backbone
Prior BENCHMARKS contrasted in Table 1 (none score policy adherence): MiniWoB++ (104 tasks), Mind2Web (2,350), WebVoyager (643), WebArena (812), VisualWebArena (910), WorkArena (29), WebCanvas (542)
